1: Four officials are named who heard Jeremiah's words: Shephatiah son of Mattan, Gedaliah son of Pashhur, Jucal (Jehucal) son of Shelemiah, and Pashhur son of Malchiah. Two of these (Jucal/Jehucal and Pashhur) were previously mentioned as envoys of Zedekiah (Jeremiah 37:3; Jeremiah 21:1). They now turn from messengers to accusers — the officials who once sought Jeremiah's help are now seeking his death.
2: הַיֹּשֵׁב בָּעִיר הַזֹּאת יָמוּת (hayoshev ba'ir hazot yamut, whoever stays in this city will die): Jeremiah's message is stark — the city is under covenant-curse. הַנֹּפֵל אֶל-הַכַּשְׂדִּים יִחְיֶה (hannofeil el-hakhashdim yichyeh, whoever goes over to the Chaldeans will live): the same nafal el (fall to/defect) vocabulary of Jeremiah 37 — what got Jeremiah arrested is now presented as the survival strategy. The triple death-by-triad: sword, famine, pestilence (cherev, ra'av, dever) for those who stay; life for those who go over.
3: הִנָּתֵן תִּנָּתֵן (hinaten tinaten, she will certainly be handed over): emphatic infinitive absolute construction expressing absolute certainty. The grammatical stress on inevitability is characteristic of Jeremiah's judgment speeches — the time for conditional clauses has passed; YHWH's word is now simply declarative.
4: מְרַפֶּא הוּא אֶת-יְדֵי אַנְשֵׁי הַמִּלְחָמָה (merappe hu et-yedei anshei hamilchama, he is weakening the hands of the soldiers): the charge uses military/political language — Jeremiah's preaching is not heresy but sedition, undermining military morale (yedei, literally 'the hands' = military capacity). דֹּרֵשׁ לְשָׁלוֹם (doresh leshalom, seeking/desiring the welfare): the accusation implies Jeremiah is a collaborator; the officials have inverted the prophetic vocation — the prophet who seeks the true welfare of the people is accused of seeking their harm.
5: הִנֵּה הוּא בְיֶדְכֶם (hineh hu beyedkhem, he is in your hands): Zedekiah's capitulation — the royal phrase of abdication. אֵין הַמֶּלֶךְ יוּכַל אֶתְכֶם דָּבָר (ein hammelekh yukhal etkhem davar, the king cannot do anything against you): a remarkable admission of the king's powerlessness against his officials. This is the theological problem of Zedekiah's reign: a man who secretly fears YHWH (vv16-17) but is politically paralyzed by his officials.
6: בּוֹר (bor, cistern/pit): the same word as Jeremiah 37's prison (beit ha'asur = house of detention → bor = actual pit). The cistern was used for water storage; when empty (ein bo mayim, no water in it) it became a prison. וַיִּשְׁקַע יִרְמְיָהוּ בַּטִּיט (vayishqa Yirmeyahu batit, Jeremiah sank into the mud): the imagery of Psalms 40:2 — 'He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog' — is realized literally. The prophet of judgment experiences covenant-curse conditions first-hand.
7: עֶבֶד-מֶלֶךְ הַכּוּשִׁי (Eved-Melekh haKushi, Ebed-melech the Ethiopian/Cushite): the name means literally 'servant of the king.' The irony is layered: a foreigner (Cushite, from sub-Saharan Africa), whose name means 'servant of the king,' serves the king of Judah while the Judean officials are murdering the king's — and ultimately YHWH's — prophet. סָרִיס (saris, court official/eunuch): the same term for Joseph's Egyptian master (Genesis 39:1). A marginalized foreigner whose service to YHWH's prophet will be honored with a specific personal oracle (Jeremiah 39:15-18).
8: Ebed-melech goes directly from the cistern to the king — the narrative pace accelerates. The bureaucratic geography (cistern → king's house) maps the power asymmetry: Ebed-melech, though low in status, has access to the king that he uses on behalf of the imprisoned prophet.
9: הָרֵעוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה (hare'u ha'anashim ha'elleh, these men have done evil): Ebed-melech's accusation before the king uses the covenant-ethics vocabulary — ra' (evil). The practical argument: Jeremiah will die of hunger (ra'av, famine — the word from the covenant-curse triad) before any military action kills him. Even in prison, the covenant curses pursue.
10: Zedekiah's command: take thirty men (shloshim anashim). The number is strikingly large — it suggests either great concern for Jeremiah's safety against opposition, or the physical difficulty of the rescue from the deep cistern.
11: סְחָבוֹת בָּלוֹת וּמְלָחִים (sechabot balot umelachim, worn-out rags and scraps): the mundane materials of Jeremiah's rescue — old rags and worn garments to cushion the rope. The physical detail is preserved because it humanizes the rescue: a powerful court official using worn cloth to save a prophet from a muddy pit. The covenantal imagination sees grace in small provisions.
12: שִׂים נָא הַסְּחָבוֹת וְהַמְּלָחִים תַּחַת אַצִּלוֹת יָדֶיךָ (sim na hassechabot vehammelachim tachat atzilot yadekha, put the rags and cloths under your armpits beneath the ropes): physical tenderness in a life-or-death rescue. Ebed-melech's care for the prophet's physical comfort (preventing rope-burn) is noted — the detail reveals a character who does good thoroughly, not minimally.
13: יַעֲלוּ אֶת-יִרְמְיָהוּ בַּחֲבָלִים (ya'alu et-Yirmeyahu bachavalim, they pulled Jeremiah up with ropes): the reversal of v6 — thrown in by officials who use their power for death; pulled out by a foreign servant who uses his access for life. Jeremiah returns to the court of the guard (chatzer hamattarah), the intermediate confinement.
14: בַּמָּבוֹא הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (bammavo hashlishi, the third entrance): the private, oblique approach — Zedekiah summons Jeremiah to an out-of-the-way entrance for a secret conversation. The secrecy is itself significant: a king who cannot publicly acknowledge the prophet whose words he privately desires.
15: הֲמוֹת תְּמִיתֵנִי (hamot temiteni, you will certainly put me to death): emphatic infinitive absolute. Jeremiah pre-empts the risk — his counsel cannot be honest unless he has immunity. The conditional structure (if I tell you ... if I counsel you ...) reveals the trap: the king needs the prophet's word but cannot protect the prophet from his own officials.
16: חַי-יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה-לָנוּ אֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַזֹּאת (chai-YHWH asher asah-lanu et-hannefesh hazot, as YHWH lives who made this life for us): an oath formula invoking YHWH as both witness and life-giver. Nefesh (life/breath/being) in YHWH's creative provision — the same root as nishmat chayyim (breath of life) in Genesis 2:7. Zedekiah's oath is the most theologically weighted statement in the chapter: he knows YHWH, swears by him, but cannot serve him.
17: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי צְבָאוֹת אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (YHWH Elohei Tzvaot Elohei Yisrael): the full throne-room title — YHWH of Hosts, God of Israel. The gravitas of the divine name proportionate to the gravity of the choice. יָצֹא תֵצֵא (yatzo tetze, if you go out): the choice Jeremiah presents is shuv (turn/surrender) and live, or refuse and be burned (vv8,18).
18: The covenant consequences of refusal: the city burned (nitzteta ba'esh), personal capture (lo timalet miyad). The warnings of Jeremiah 21 and 34 are recapitulated here in direct address to the king. The personal dimension is sharpened — Zedekiah is not just warned about the city but told exactly what will happen to him personally.
19: יָרֵאתִי (yare'ti, I am afraid): the royal confession of fear. The king who cannot submit to Babylon fears a third party — the Judean defectors (hanefashim asher nafelu el-hakhashdim). The same nafal el idiom. Political paralysis layered on fear: afraid of Nebuchadnezzar, afraid of the defectors, afraid of his own officials — and therefore unable to act on the word of YHWH.
20: שְׁמַע בְּקוֹל יְהוָה (shema beqol YHWH, obey the voice of YHWH): the Deuteronomic obedience formula — shama' beqol is the covenantal call to hear-and-obey. Jeremiah's promise: וְיִיטַב לְךָ (veyitav lekha, and it will go well with you) — the blessing side of the covenant. The offer of life is still available, even at this late hour.
21: The adversative condition: וְאִם-מָאֵן אַתָּה (ve'im-maen attah, but if you refuse): the hinge between blessing and curse. YHWH has revealed (hirani YHWH) what will happen if Zedekiah refuses. Prophetic revelation gives exact knowledge of covenant consequences.
22: הֻצְאוּ כָּל-הַנָּשִׁים (hutzu kol-hannashim, all the women will be led out): the women of the royal court, Zedekiah's dependents, will be taken to the Babylonian officials. הֶחֱלִיאֻךָ וְיָכְלוּ לְךָ אַנְשֵׁי שְׁלֹמֶךָ (hecheli'ukha veyakhlu lekha anshei shlomekha, your trusted men have prevailed against you and defeated you): the taunt-song the women will sing exposes the social reality — the king was destroyed by the very men who should have supported him.
23: מַלֵּט לֹא תִמָּלֵט (mallet lo timaleit, you will not escape at all): emphatic double infinitive absolute — absolute certainty of capture. תִּתָּפֵשׂ (titafes, you will be seized): the fulfillment comes in Jeremiah 39:5 — word becomes event. The prophecy is not threat but description of inevitable consequence.
24: Zedekiah's response to prophetic counsel: not acceptance or rejection but a demand for secrecy. אַל-יֵדַע אִישׁ (al-yeda ish, let no man know): the king's primary concern is political exposure, not personal survival through obedience.
25: The scenario Zedekiah anticipates: officials questioning Jeremiah about the content of the private conversation. The king provides a cover story — a plausible official reason for the meeting (Jeremiah was petitioning about his imprisonment).
26: The cover story: אֲנִי מַפִּיל תְּחִנָּתִי לִפְנֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ (ani mappil techinnati lifnei hammelekh, I was laying my plea before the king): techinnah (entreaty/petition) is the word Jeremiah used in 37:20 for his actual request about imprisonment. The official story is a partial truth that conceals the whole truth. The episode raises the ethical complexity of prophetic survival in an authoritarian environment.
27: וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ אֹתוֹ (vayyish'alu oto, they questioned him): the officials do exactly what Zedekiah predicted. Jeremiah's answer follows the king's script. וַיֶּחֱשׁוּ מִמֶּנּוּ (vayechashu mimmenu, they were silent/stopped questioning him): the cover holds. The political survival of the prophet is secured — but the theological problem remains: the word that would have saved the city is suppressed.
28: עַד-הַיּוֹם אֲשֶׁר-נִלְכְּדָה יְרוּשָׁלָ͏ִם (ad-hayyom asher-nilkedeha Yerushalayim, until the day Jerusalem was captured): the understated narrative close. Jeremiah's confinement continues until the city falls. The final clause of the verse in some manuscripts links directly to Jeremiah 39 — the prophet in the court of the guard when Jerusalem is captured.
